The transpiration method was used to equilibrate dilute gaseous mixtures of HF and $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$ in hydrogen carrier gas with molten LiF-BeF<sub>2</sub> mixtures varying in composition from 0.25 to 0.80 BeF<sub>2</sub>, both saturated and unsaturated with crystalline BeO, in the temperature range 500 to $700^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ .
The partial pressure data were used to evaluate the equilibrium quotient for the reaction of HF and $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$ with solid BeO and dissolved BeF<sub>2</sub>.

These equilibrium quotients were employed to determine: (1) thermodynamic activities of LiF and $\mathsf{BeF_2}$ in the mixtures; (2) thermodynamics of liquid $\mathsf{BeF_2}$ ; (3) stability of hydroxide in the melt; and (4) solubility of BeO in the LiF- $\mathsf{BeF_2}$ system as a function of temperature.
According to Everest, $^1$ many of the investigations of beryllium fluoride systems did not take into account the role of moisture and the resulting hydrolysis products.

From such a study information could in principle be obtained about: (1) the thermodynamic activities of LiF and BeF $_2$ , (2) the solubilities and stabilities of oxides, and (3) the interaction of oxide with the proton and perhaps other cations in this molten fluoride system.

In the present study the transpiration method was used to equilibrate dilute gaseous mixtures of HF and $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$ in hydrogen carrier gas with molten LiF-BeF $_2$ mixtures varying in composition from 0.25 to 0.80 BeF $_2$ , both saturated and unsaturated with crystalline BeO, in the temperature range 500 to $700^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ .

For unsaturated melts, $Q_{0}$ is based on the difference between oxygen material balance and proton material balance. $Q_{0}$ is subject to the same corrections as $Q_{A}$ , hence it is from 1.0% high to 0.25% low.
The precision of weighing and transferring samples to the vessel was about $\pm 0.2\%$ .
in analyses of successive samples from the same melt was generally $\pm 4\%$ for each constituent, which was clearly greater than the uncertainty based on weights of components.
Determination of moisture content of typical samples by the thermogravimetric method indicated 0.1 and $0.4\%$ moisture, respectively.
Thus, on the basis of the above uncertainties in weight and moisture content, the melt compositions, expressed as mole fraction $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ , were probably known within $\pm 0.5\%$ .

The amounts of the impurities Cr, Cu, Fe, Ni and S were determined in the starting materials, in filtered samples of the melt after it had been sparged with H $_2$ for more than 48 hr, and again immediately after sparging for 6 hr with a dilute mixture of HF and H $_2$ O in H $_2$ .
All analyses were within the following range: Cr always less than 20 ppm, Cu always less than 100 ppm, Fe usually between 100 and 200 ppm, Ni usually less than 20 ppm, and S always less than 5 ppm.
Total measured impurities only amount to about 350 ppm which is not significant in terms of melt composition.

Consumption of HF would have occurred if sulfide had been present in concentrations greater than 5 ppm by the reaction of $\mathbf{S}^{2-}$ with HF in a manner analogous to the reaction of oxide with HF.[47] Since the impurity levels were not appreciably decreased during the $\mathrm{H}_2$ sparging, the impurities apparently were not reduced to the metallic state.
The results of Blood's study on the stability of the difluorides of nickel, iron and chromium[21] indicate they should eventually be reduced.

His results indicate that: the Ni reaction vessel would not react with HF to produce NiF $_2$ concentrations greater than 0.1 ppm with the highest $(\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{HF}})^2 /\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{H}_2}$ ratios employed here; the Cr would have been in the CrF $_2$ state for all $(\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{HF}})^2 /\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{H}_2}$ ratios above 1 x $10^{-8}$ (true at all times except for extended stripping with H $_2$ ); the FeF $_2$ concentration could have varied between the time when H $_2$ was used and when $(\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{HF}})^2 /\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{H}_2} = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ atm.
Anhydrous HF- $\mathsf{H}_2$ mixtures (approximately 0.01 atm HF) were employed to remove oxide and hydroxide from melt.
The total amount of HF stripped out was about 5 ppm and was probably due to HF solubility.
Melt Temperature---The temperature of the melt varied about $\pm 0.5^{\circ}$ during the furnace heating and cooling cycle.
The observed temperature with the thermocouple at different depths in the melt did not vary more than $\pm 1.0^{\circ}$ .
Overall, the temperature of a melt was known and constant to $\pm 1.5^{\circ}$ .
In this case the temperature read about 15 to $20^{\circ}$ lower at complete insertion of the thermocouple than when the thermocouple was withdrawn about 1 in. from the bottom.
Even in this case the temperature varied no more than $\pm 2^{\circ}$ when the thermocouple was withdrawn further.
Titer Precision.--A 25-liter reservoir of KOH was standardized with potassium acid phthalate before initial use and again approximately 3 and 6 months later.
All values agreed with the average value $\pm 0.1\%$ .
The 150-ml reservoir of the Karl Fischer apparatus was filled as needed from quart bottles of Karl Fischer Reagent.
Water (0.050 to 0.080 g) was added directly to the titration vessel from a weighing buret and the Karl Fischer titer determined.
Successive titrations agreed within ± 0.3%.
Usually the concentration change from one standardization to the next would not exceed 1%.
The precision in measuring the concentration of Karl Fischer Reagent was ± 0.5%.
Wet-test Meter Temperature.--The wet-test meter temperature, which was the temperature of the gas as its volume was being measured, varied no more than $\pm 0.5^{\circ}$ during a given experiment.
Due to the variation in temperature, the variation in measured volume is about $\pm 0.2\%$ , including the effect due to the change in vapor pressure of water.
Flow-rate Precision.--The precision in the timing of flow rates was 0.1 second per $100\mathrm{ml}$ .
For a typical value of 50 seconds per $100\mathrm{ml}$ , this would amount to $0.2\%$ .

The plot of activities, which should be proportional to the partial pressures of $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ and LiF, corresponds to the predicted behavior of a system showing incomplete miscibility.[52] Since the positive deviations increase with temperature, these data seem to indicate that the system has a lower consolute temperature near $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 0.80 $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ .

To date there is no clearcut evidence of partial immiscibility in the LiF-BeF $_2$ system although various investigators have considered the possibility of such occurrence.[53-55] The MgO-SiO $_2$ system, which has frequently been compared with the LiF-BeF $_2$ system,[1] indeed does show a miscibility gap.
